%% The abstract is a short summary of the work to be presented in the
%% article.
\begin{abstract}
Robotic manipulation has become a central problem in embodied intelligence, especially in the era of vision-language-action models, world models, and diffusion-based policies. These advances have broadened the design space of learned manipulation systems and increased the need to analyze embodiment, data, learning, integration, and evaluation together.
This survey organizes learned manipulation intelligence for robotic arms along four dimensions: a three-tier data regime, a dual-pathway learning decomposition into cognitive planning and policy learning, three integration architectures, and four evaluation axes. This structure makes it easier to compare how different parts of the stack interact and where recurring design pressures arise.
From this perspective, the survey examines how embodiment shapes data and control interfaces, how data regimes affect learning and integration choices, and how deployment and evaluation are linked in practical embodied systems. Finally, we conclude with open challenges in heterogeneous data transfer, cross-embodiment generalization, and system-level trustworthiness.
\end{abstract}

\section{Introduction}\label{sec:introduction}

Embodied intelligence views cognition as arising from the dynamic coupling of brain, body, and environment, rather than as a purely disembodied computational process~\cite{clarkBeingTherePutting1998, brooksIntelligenceWithoutRepresentation1991}.
Robotic manipulation provides a particularly challenging and revealing setting for examining this view, as it requires joint reasoning over contact dynamics, force exchange, high-DoF kinematics, and fine-grained object perception~\cite{khatibInertialPropertiesRobot1987}.
These characteristics have established robotic arms as a key platform for embodied intelligence research, whose importance has grown further with advances in deep learning and, more recently, foundation models and LLMs. 
Recent progress in vision-language-action models, world models, and diffusion-based policies has significantly enriched the range of approaches for robotic manipulation. 
Several recent surveys cover adjacent parts of this landscape, including unified abstractions for robotic manipulation~\cite{baiUnifiedUnderstandingRobot2025}, foundation-model components in real-world robotics~\cite{kawaharazukaVisionLanguageActionModelsRobotics2025}, object-centric embodied learning~\cite{survey2025objectcentric}, and broader embodied AI systems built on LLMs and world models~\cite{liuEmbodiedIntelligenceSynergy2025}. 
These surveys provide valuable coverage, but many of them organize the area primarily by model family or at a relatively broad level of abstraction.
For manipulation, categories such as VLA and RL can sometimes obscure substantial differences in planning artifacts, action representations, and system integration. 
Data should be treated more centrally in manipulation research, since collection regime, embodiment heterogeneity, and pipeline design directly affect what learning and integration choices are practical. 
This survey therefore organizes the field around data regimes, cognitive planning, policy learning, and integration architectures and examines learned manipulation intelligence across robotic arm platforms, including stationary, bimanual, mobile, and humanoid configurations, with emphasis on how embodiment, data, learning, and evaluation jointly shape manipulation capability.

\textbf{Contributions:}
This survey makes three contributions.
First, it introduces a layered taxonomy of embodied datasets and analyzes the current data production ecosystem, showing how acquisition settings, simulation environments, and large-scale data pipelines collectively generate and sustain the datasets and benchmarks used in modern robotic manipulation.
Second, it distinguishes cognitive planning from policy learning, and analyzes how different learning paradigms fit into this decomposition, how they are integrated into complete manipulation systems, and what deployment trade-offs they entail.
Third, it examines embodied manipulation as a coupled design problem, analyzing how embodiment constraints, data regimes, learning architectures, deployment requirements, and evaluation protocols interact, and how these components jointly shape system design, deployment behavior, and real-world performance.

\textbf{Structure of the Paper:}
Section~\ref{sec:embodiment_hardware} reviews physical embodiment and software infrastructure. Section~\ref{sec:embodiment_dataset} examines the data ecosystem from datasets through acquisition, synthesis, refinement, and pipelines. Section~\ref{sec:embodiment_method} analyzes cognitive planning, policy learning, integration models, and deployment. Section~\ref{sec:evaluation} synthesizes evaluation methodologies, and Section~\ref{sec:challenges} concludes with open challenges and future directions. Figure~\ref{fig:survey_structure} summarizes this organization.

  \centering

\section{Embodiment Foundations of Robotic Arms}\label{sec:embodiment_hardware}

For robotic arm systems, embodiment foundations defines both the physical substrate of interaction and the computational substrate of control, thereby setting the conditions under which perception, action, and coordination can be realized.
This section therefore examines embodiment as the joint basis of hardware capability and system support, spanning physical embodiment and the software framework.

\subsection{Physical Embodiment}

Physical embodiment shapes robotic manipulation before learning starts. 
In robotic arm systems, it governs how contact can be made, and how these conditions can be converted into controllable behavior.  
We begin at the manipulation interface, through end effectors and sensors, and then at the arm level, where multi-DoF motion structures contact into executable manipulation.
Figure~\ref{fig:embodiment_taxonomy} summarizes this taxonomy. 
From this perspective, physical embodiment can be seen as the source of the constraints that define manipulation objectives, data requirements, and methodological choices.

\subsubsection{End Effectors and Sensors}\label{subsec:end_effectors}

End effectors shape the contact space of manipulation by determining how contact is formed, maintained, and transformed during interaction.
Different end-effector designs therefore differ not only in capability, but in the contact regime through which manipulation is organized.
Jaw grippers, such as the Robotiq 2F/3F series, favor stable and mechanically constrained contact, making grasping, alignment, and release easier to control and predict.
Dexterous hands, such as the Shadow Hand and Allegro Hand, allow contacts to change more freely during manipulation and support richer in-hand interaction, but they also make behavior harder to reduce to a small set of repeatable contact patterns.
Soft hands, including the RBO Hand 3, Festo BionicSoftHand, and SpiRobs, address uncertainty differently by using compliance to absorb part of the interaction variability in the hardware itself.

Sensors shape the observation space of manipulation by providing access to task-relevant information during execution.
The central task of sensors is to ensure the information required at different stages of interaction is observable.
Before contact, the dominant uncertainties concern object geometry, pose, and scene layout, so visual sensing is often sufficient for coarse localization, alignment, and approach. 
During contact, however, task-relevant information becomes increasingly local and interaction-dependent, including slip, deformation, insertion error, and force misalignment. 
These states are often critical to task success, yet they are only weakly reflected in external visual observations. 
For this reason, tactile, force, and proprioceptive sensing become critical once task-relevant information is expressed primarily through contact itself. 
Sensors such as GelSight, DIGIT, and IMU-equipped end effectors make different portions of this observation space accessible by exposing local contact geometry, contact transients, and motion signals that are difficult to recover from external vision alone.

End effectors and sensors shape how a robotic arm interacts with its environment. 
Through contact space and observation space, they bound the otherwise open-ended complexity of manipulation into structured constraints, making the discussion that follows more tractable.

\subsubsection{Robotic arms}\label{subsec:manipulators}

Robotic arms organize manipulation at the execution level by transforming contact and observation into controllable multi-DoF motion. Mounted on fixed bases, they provide controllable multi-DoF motion that allows contact to be established, maintained, and transformed during execution. 
In this sense, robotic manipulation is defined not by motion alone, but by how motion is organized around contact under partial observability and task constraints.

In single-arm settings, such as the Franka Panda, KUKA iiwa, and Kinova Gen3, the structure of execution is relatively localized. 
Manipulation therefore can be organized around a relatively compact set of objectives: establishing a stable grasp, aligning the object to a desired pose, and completing the contact transition. 
What matters here is not simply whether the arm can move, but whether its motion can produce reliable end-effector behavior under uncertainty. 
Reachability, local contact regulation, and pose-sensitive execution therefore become central. 
This is why single-arm systems continue to serve as the dominant setting for teleoperation, policy benchmarking, and high-precision force-controlled manipulation.

Bimanual settings, such as dual Franka Panda setups and ABB YuMi, reorganize these objectives rather than simply extending them. 
Once two manipulators act on the same object, success is no longer determined solely by local grasp stability or end-effector precision. 
Instead, the task becomes one of coordinated object transformation: 
relative arm motion must keep consistent, 
internal forces must keep consistent with the object and the environment, 
and temporary closed-chain constraints may arise during interaction. 
As a result, the emphasis shifts from localized intervention to coordinated execution, where timing, force sharing, and multi-contact feasibility become intrinsic to the task.

What distinguishes these regimes is not simply the number of manipulators, but the structure they impose on manipulation. 
Single-arm systems keep the problem comparatively localized, whereas bimanual systems reorganize it around coordinated object transformation. 
This distinction matters beyond platform description. 
It determines the interactions that later chapters must cover in data, the dependencies that the methodology must represent, and the coordination assumptions that control must preserve during execution.

\subsubsection{Mobile Platforms}\label{subsec:mobile_platforms}

Mounting robotic arms on mobile platforms expands the scope of manipulation.
In wheeled mobile manipulators such as PR2, TIAGo, and Stretch, arm action must already be coordinated with base motion and navigation constraints. 
In legged platforms and humanoids, including Spot-based systems, Atlas, Figure~02, Optimus, and Unitree G1, this coupling becomes stronger still: manipulation must be executed together with balance, posture regulation, and system-level support conditions. 
In conclusion, the operation of robotic arms, as a central component of robotic manipulation, is increasingly shaped by mobility, balance, and whole-body support conditions.

\begin{figure}[t]
    \centering
    \includegraphics[width=\linewidth,trim=0cm 4.1cm 0cm 5.9cm,clip]{assets/embodiment.pdf}
    \caption{Overview of physical embodiments for robotic manipulation. The figure covers end effectors, sensors, stationary platforms, and mobile platforms.}

End effectors are classified by contact modality (jaw gripper, dexterous hand, soft hand) and sensing (vision, tactile); platforms are divided into stationary (single-arm, bimanual) and mobile (wheeled, quadrupedal, humanoid).}\label{fig:embodiment_taxonomy}
\end{figure}

\subsection{Software Framework}

\begin{table}[t]

The software stack is organized into three layers. L0 is the Kernel Layer for hard real-time motor control with microsecond-level determinism. L1 is the Transport Layer for high-throughput, low-latency inter-process and inter-device communication. L2 is the System Layer for distributed orchestration across robots, edge devices, cloud resources, and fleets. The Profile column reports interrupt or IPC latency for L0 and L1 systems, and deployment or orchestration characteristics for L2 systems.}\label{tab:framework_comparison}

If physical embodiment determines what a robotic arm can do, the software framework determines how those capabilities are organized and executed at runtime.
We therefore turn from embodiment as hardware to embodiment as system support, focusing on the middleware, communication, and control layers that make robotic manipulation deployable in practice.

\subsubsection{Modern Robotics Middleware and Operating Systems}\label{subsec:control_middleware}

The software stack of embodied manipulation systems must support real-time control, efficient data transport, and system-level orchestration across heterogeneous compute and hardware. 
We organize this stack into three tiers, as summarized in Table~\ref{tab:framework_comparison}. 

The Kernel Layer (L0) centers on temporal determinism and low-latency control execution. Industrial stacks rely on kernels such as VxWorks and QNX for bounded interrupt latency and fault isolation, while Zephyr extends the same guarantees to resource-constrained edge devices.
The Transport Layer (L1) is responsible for moving data between processes, devices, and model components, and today relies heavily on ROS~2, with decentralized DDS replacing earlier centralized designs~\cite{macenski2022robot}. 
For embodied models, the pressure now comes less from basic connectivity than from the cost of moving multimodal data, which has driven zero-copy dataflow designs such as Dora-rs~\cite{zhang2026dora}.
The System Layer (L2) shifts the emphasis from local message passing to orchestration across robots, edge devices, cloud services, and fleets. Systems such as Zenoh-Flow and FogROS2 treat deployment, routing, and compute offloading as first-class concerns, while RoboOS represents a more recent AI-native direction in which high-level multimodal planning is coupled to reusable skill libraries across heterogeneous embodiments~\cite{baldoni2023zenohflow, ichnowski2022fogros2, wang2025roboos}.

\subsubsection{Low-level Motion Planning and Control}\label{subsec:lowlevel_control}

While middleware provides the communication substrate, the low-level motion generation and control layer translates task-space references into physically executable joint commands. 
Its core role is to satisfy several tightly coupled requirements simultaneously: mapping between operational and joint space, compensating nonlinear rigid-body dynamics, enforcing joint and collision constraints, and maintaining stability under external contact. 
For this reason, embodied systems commonly adopt optimization-based controllers, particularly model predictive control and whole-body control, which solve tracking, feasibility, and safety objectives within a unified constrained optimization problem solved online at the control rate~\cite{schulman2014optimizing}.

In manipulation, contact behavior is as important as free-space accuracy. 
Impedance control is a standard mechanism for shaping compliant interaction during assembly, tool use, and human-robot collaboration by balancing positional deviations through compliance and tuned stiffness.
In practice, however, the effectiveness of MPC, WBC, and impedance control still depends on low-level temporal determinism: timing jitter in the execution loop can destabilize high-frequency feedback well before perception or learning becomes the limiting factor.

Together, hardware embodiment and the software stack define the physical and computational constraints under which any learned policy must operate. 
These constraints shape system execution and define the data requirements for learning. The next question, therefore, is what data enables effective learning under such constraints, and how that data should be structured, collected, and scaled.

\section{Data Ecosystem of Robotic Arms}\label{sec:embodiment_dataset}

\begin{figure}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.75\textwidth,trim=0cm 0.9cm 1.5cm 0.5cm,clip]{assets/datasupport.pdf}
  \caption{The taxonomy organizes Section 3 around embodied datasets, data acquisition, data refinement, and data pipelines, showing how data is collected, synthesized, improved, and scaled to support downstream learning and deployment.}
  \Description{A hierarchical diagram showing the organization of the survey on embodied AI for robotic arms. The top node branches into four main pillars: system architecture, data ecosystem, architectural paradigms, and evaluation metrics. These pillars further expand into subtopics including embodied systems, dataset categories, data refinement, cognitive planning, policy learning, integration models, deployment lifecycle, and evaluation criteria. Arrows indicate conceptual dependencies across the survey structure.}\label{fig:data_support}
\end{figure}

Having clarified how embodiment constrains robotic manipulation through contact space, observation space, and arm-centered execution, we now turn to the data ecosystem through which these constraints become learnable. 
In robotic manipulation, we denote this data support by \(\mathcal{D}\): a structured record of embodied execution, including sequences of motion, state transitions, sensory observations, and contact events generated as the robot interacts with the world. 
Progress therefore depends not only on model design, but also on how \(\mathcal{D}\) is organized, collected, refined, and scaled across tasks, scenes, and embodiments. 
This chapter begins with embodied datasets in Section~\ref{sec:eai_datasets} and then examines the broader processes centered on them, including data acquisition in Section~\ref{sec:data_acquisition}, data refinement in Section~\ref{sec:data_refinement}, and the large-scale pipelines for datasets in Section~\ref{sec:data_factories_robotic_arms}.
Together, these elements provide a structured view of the support system summarized in Fig.~\ref{fig:data_support}.

\subsection{Embodied Datasets}\label{sec:eai_datasets}

Through a systematic review of the literature, we observe that embodied manipulation datasets have evolved toward progressively broader support for learning, from preserving local interaction structure (Table~\ref{tab:skill_datasets}), to capturing how robotic arms organize these interactions into temporally extended execution (Table~\ref{tab:task_datasets}), and ultimately to supporting transfer across heterogeneous embodiments (Table~\ref{tab:foundation_datasets}).
We summarize this progression as a transition from \(\mathcal{D}_{i-1}\) to \(\mathcal{D}_i\), where each tier enlarges the available data support and thereby expands the effective knowledge support \(\mathcal{K}\) available to the system. 
Concretely, skill-level datasets \(\mathcal{D}_1\) provide support at the level of \(\mathcal{K}_1\) by encoding information about local interaction dynamics, 
task-level datasets \(\mathcal{D}_2\) provide support at the level of \(\mathcal{K}_2\) by encoding information about the composition of skills into temporally extended behaviors, 
and foundation-level datasets \(\mathcal{D}_3\) provide support at the level of \(\mathcal{K}_3\) by encoding the cross-embodiment variation needed for transfer at scale.

Note the scarcity of real-world data for non-grasping primitives.}\label{tab:skill_datasets}

\textbf{Dex1B}~\cite{ye2025dex1b} & 2025 & Dexterous Grasp & Single Object & 1M & Sim & 1B grasps & Point Cloud & $\times$ \\
\midrule
\multicolumn{9}{c}{\textbf{Language-Grounded Grasping}} \\
\midrule

\textbf{ReasoningGrasp}~\cite{jin2024reasoninggrasp} & 2024 & 6-DoF Grasp & Multi-Object & 64 & Real & 99M grasps & RGB-D & $\checkmark$ \\
\midrule
\multicolumn{9}{c}{\textbf{Diverse Motor Primitives}} \\
\midrule
\textbf{Omnipush}~\cite{bauza2021omnipush} & 2019 & Pushing (Planar) & Single Object & 250 & Real & 62.5k pushes & RGB-D & $\times$ \\
\textbf{PartNet-Mobility}~\cite{xiang2020sapien} & 2020 & Articulated (Open/Close) & Single Object & 2,346 models & Sim & 2,346 articulated assets & 3D / PC & $\times$ \\
\textbf{FurnitureBench}~\cite{heo2023furniturebench} & 2023 & Assembly (Precise) & Tabletop & Furniture & Sim+Real & 5.1k demos (220 hrs) & RGB & $\times$ \\

\end{tabular}
}
\end{table}

\subsubsection{Skill-level Datasets}\label{sec:skill_datasets_narrative}

The development of skill-level datasets reflects a steady expansion of \(\mathcal{D}_1\), from planar grasp annotations, to dense 6-DoF grasp synthesis, to dexterous hand configurations and non-grasping interactions such as pushing, articulation, and assembly. 
Table~\ref{tab:skill_datasets} lists representative datasets along this tier.
Early grasping benchmarks such as Cornell~\cite{lenz2014deep} labeled graspable regions as 2D oriented rectangles, covering only top-down parallel-jaw approaches.
Dex-Net 2.0~\cite{mahler2017dex} used analytical force-closure synthesis to generate millions of grasp labels without physical trials.
GraspNet-1B~\cite{fang2020graspnet} annotated 1.2 billion 6-DoF grasps across cluttered real-world scenes, and GraspClutter6D~\cite{back2025graspclutter6d} extended this to denser clutter configurations.
For multi-fingered hands ($>$20 DoF), DexGraspNet 2.0~\cite{zhang2024dexgraspnet20learninggenerative} synthesized 427M grasps via diffusion-based generation (90.7\% real-world success), and Dex1B~\cite{ye2025dex1b} reached one billion grasps.
Tactile modalities complement vision: T-DEX~\cite{guzey2023tdex} showed that 2.5 hours of self-supervised tactile play data improves dexterous task success by 1.7$\times$ over vision-only baselines, capturing slip and reorientation signals invisible to cameras.
Beyond grasping, Omnipush~\cite{bauza2021omnipush} records planar pushing dynamics across 250 objects, PartNet-Mobility~\cite{xiang2020sapien} provides part-level interaction data for articulated objects, and FurnitureBench~\cite{heo2023furniturebench} supplies 5,000 teleoperated assembly trajectories with a paired simulator.

\subsubsection{Task-level Datasets}\label{sec:task_datasets_narrative}

Task-level datasets evolved \(\mathcal{D}_2\) by extending isolated motor primitives into instruction-aligned trajectories, gradually increasing semantic coverage, environmental diversity, and calibration quality across collection sites. Table~\ref{tab:task_datasets} lists representative datasets along this tier.
MT-Opt~\cite{kalashnikov2021mt} collected 800k trajectories through autonomous exploration but used task IDs to index actions, without language supervision. 
BC-Z~\cite{jang2022bc} introduced language-conditioned supervision by pairing 26k trajectories with 100 semantic verbs, and RT-1 Dataset~\cite{brohan2023rt1} extended this to 130k trajectories spanning 700+ instructions. 
These early datasets were collected in one or two institutional kitchens, limiting environmental diversity. 
BridgeData V2~\cite{walke2024bridgedatav2} widened scene coverage to 24 environments via multi-site collection, DROID~\cite{khazatsky2024droid} coordinated 18 institutions across 564 scenes with calibrated extrinsics, and Dobb-E~\cite{shafiullah2023bringing} accessed 22 domestic environments using only an iPhone mounted on a Stretch robot. 
FMB~\cite{fu2025fmb} added force-torque calibration across 22k contact-rich assembly trajectories.

\subsubsection{Foundation-level Datasets}\label{sec:foundation_datasets}

Foundation-level datasets expand \(\mathcal{D}_3\) by organizing robot experience across platforms into a unified layer of support for cross-embodiment learning, with increasing emphasis on alignment, standardization, and multimodal consistency.
Table~\ref{tab:foundation_datasets} summarizes representative datasets in this tier.
RoboNet~\cite{dasari2020robonet} established an early multi-robot dataset with 15 million video frames collected across seven robot platforms.
OXE~\cite{padalkar2023open} scaled this direction by unifying over one million trajectories from 22 robot platforms through the RLDS protocol.
As this tier scales, standardization and multimodal alignment become equally important.
ARIO~\cite{wang2024ario} provides a unified large-scale dataset with approximately three million episodes and consistent timestamp alignment across heterogeneous sources, while RH-20T~\cite{fang2023rh} contributes over 110k contact-rich manipulation sequences with synchronized visual, force, audio, and action signals.
RoboMIND~\cite{wu2025robomind} extends this tier toward standardized multi-robot task datasets, pairing 107k trajectories across four robot types with failure annotations, and RoboMIND 2.0~\cite{hou2026robomind20} scales this to over 310k trajectories across six robot platforms and 739 tasks.
AgiBot World Beta~\cite{agibot2025} pushes this tier toward foundation-scale humanoid and dual-arm manipulation, providing over one million trajectories from 100 robots across 100+ real-world scenarios.
As robot data demand outpaces collection speed, some pipelines supplement trajectory datasets with egocentric human video such as Ego-Exo4D~\cite{grauman2024egoexo4d}.

\subsection{Data Acquisition}\label{sec:data_acquisition}

The previous section distinguished dataset tiers by the support they provide. 
However, acquiring such support is not straightforward. 
Unlike generic datasets, embodied manipulation data must record action sequences under the constraints of observation space and contact space. 
Collecting data that is both physically grounded and rich enough to support learning at scale is difficult. 
This section therefore examines three main routes by which embodied interaction is converted into \(\mathcal{D}\) through real-world collection in Section~\ref{sec:real_acquisition}, simulation ecosystems in Section~\ref{sec:simulation_infrastructure}, and automatic data generation in Section~\ref{sec:auto_data_gen}. 

\subsubsection{Real-world Data Gathering}\label{sec:real_acquisition}

Since real-world collection conducts under concrete hardware and scene constraints, its efficiency and coverage are constrained by the robotic arm platform, end effectors, and execution environments. 
Recent research can be read as a gradual effort to weaken this coupling while retaining useful observation, contact, and execution structure. 
Direct teleoperation on the deployment robot provides a straightforward way to acquire such data, and it forms the basis of many early approaches.
RoboTurk~\cite{mandlekar2018roboturk} built a cloud-based teleoperation interface that enabled demonstrations to be collected from geographically distributed users over the internet rather than in a single shared lab. 
ALOHA~\cite{zhao2023learning, fu2024mobile} introduced a low-cost teleoperation setup for bimanual and later mobile whole-body manipulation, making long-horizon and contact-rich data collection more accessible. 
Teleoperation preserves the coupling between observation space, contact space, and arm-centered execution most faithfully, but it is also tightly constrained by hardware access and human labor. 
For this reason, later work seeks more reusable collection interfaces so that the resulting behavior can transfer across several robotic arm platforms. 
UMI~\cite{chi2024umi} used handheld grippers and consumer cameras to capture demonstrations in everyday environments and then retargeted them offline to the target robot platform, 
while DexCap~\cite{wang2024dexcap} pushed this approach toward dexterous manipulation by recording high-dimensional human hand motion for transfer to multi-fingered robot hands within the same broader dexterous-hand regime. 
Even so, these interfaces are limited to narrower robotic arms and still rely on teleoperation. 
This motivates a further step toward egocentric and video-based supervision by retargeting human demonstrations back into robotic arm execution.
EgoMimic~\cite{kareer2025egomimic} used egocentric human video to enable large-scale data capture without requiring robot hardware during collection, 
and AV-ALOHA~\cite{chuang2025avaloha} showed that such data become more useful when the demonstrator's viewpoint is perceptually aligned with the robot's onboard observation space. 

\subsubsection{Simulation Data Ecosystems}\label{sec:simulation_infrastructure}

\begin{table}[t]
\centering

\resizebox{\textwidth}{!}{%
\begin{tabular}{@{}l >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{4.2cm} >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{5.8cm} >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{5.5cm}@{}}
\toprule
\textbf{Simulator} & \textbf{Features} & \textbf{Capabilities \& Modalities} & \textbf{Key Benchmarks \& Datasets} \\
\midrule
\multicolumn{4}{c}{\textbf{\textbf{I. The Core Simulation Ecosystems}}} \\
\midrule
\textbf{MuJoCo} & Stable analytic contact solver; MJX hardware acceleration (TPU/GPU); Unified differentiable dynamics. & \textbf{Physics-Aligned Data}: Artifact-free trajectories for fine-grained motor control.

\midrule

\multicolumn{4}{c}{\textbf{\textbf{II\@. Specialized Task Platforms}}} \\
\midrule
\textbf{PyBullet / CoppeliaSim} & Lightweight legacy physics; Stable IK solvers; Broad hardware and ROS abstraction. & \textbf{Algorithmic Baseline Data}: Logic planning and action primitives. 

\textbf{Modalities: RGB-D, Joint states, Contact forces, Proximity sensors, Force/Torque.} & \textbf{CALVIN}~\cite{mees2022calvin}, \textbf{RLBench}~\cite{james2020rlbench} \\

\textbf{Habitat / AI2-THOR} & Procedural indoor scene generation; High-throughput visual navigation kernels. & \textbf{Embodied Navigation Data}: Semantic search and indoor reasoning. 

\textbf{Modalities: RGB-D, Segmentations, Object states, Agent pose, Audio.} & OpenEQA~\cite{majumdar2024openeqa}, ALFRED~\cite{shridhar2020alfred} \\

\bottomrule
\end{tabular}}
\end{table}

Compared with real-world collection, simulation weakens the dependence of data acquisition on specific robotic arm platforms, end-effector and sensor setups, and scene conditions. 
This makes it easier to construct different ranges of \(\mathcal{D}\) for robotic arm manipulation under clearer assumptions.
In this section, MuJoCo, SAPIEN, and NVIDIA Isaac are treated as three representative directions because they align naturally with different tiers of \(\mathcal{D}\)  for robotic arm manipulation, while other platforms serve for specialized applications. Table~\ref{tab:sim_classification} summarizes these design tendencies.
MuJoCo~\cite{todorov2012mujoco} is well suited with \(\mathcal{D}_1\) data, where stable local interaction and controller-oriented rollouts matter most for robotic arm execution.
Meta-World~\cite{yu2021meta} provided a standard state-based multi-task setting, 
and later suites such as LIBERO~\cite{liu2023libero} and RoboCasa~\cite{nasiriany2024robocasa} added lifelong-learning curricula and richer household scenes. 
MJX and MuJoCo Playground support larger-batch rollouts, differentiable simulation, and sim-to-real analysis within the same stack~\cite{deepmind2025playground}.
SAPIEN~\cite{xiang2020sapien} is particularly suitable for \(\mathcal{D}_2\) support, since articulated object states and part-level semantics make temporally extended object interaction easier to structure for robotic arms.
PartNet-Mobility provides articulated assets with annotated joints, ManiSkill builds benchmark suites on this basis~\cite{tao2025maniskill3}, 
and SIMPLER uses the same infrastructure for sim-to-real evaluation~\cite{li2025simpler}.
NVIDIA Isaac is the platform best aligned with \(\mathcal{D}_3\) particularly in settings that demand multimodality, scale, and broad platform coverage~\cite{mittal2023orbit}. 
Representative embodied benchmarks built on Isaac-Sim-based simulation stacks include BEHAVIOR-1K, which targets large-scale household activities, 
and ARNOLD, which focuses on language-grounded manipulation with rich continuous object states~\cite{li2024behavior1k, gong2023arnold}.
Beyond these three ecosystems, several other platforms still used for specialized applications of robotic arm manipulation.
PyBullet and CoppeliaSim support manipulation baselines such as RLBench and CALVIN~\cite{james2020rlbench, mees2022calvin}.
Habitat and AI2-THOR help fill scene-level semantic gaps that main manipulation simulators cover less well, supporting benchmarks such as ALFRED and OpenEQA~\cite{shridhar2020alfred, majumdar2024openeqa}.
SoftGym supports deformable object manipulation benchmarks that rigid-body simulators still capture only weakly~\cite{lin2021softgym}.
By reducing dependence on physical robot hardware, simulation improves experimental efficiency and allows more effort to be directed toward algorithmic questions above the arm hardware layer. 
However, it cannot fully reproduce real physical environments or hardware-specific effects, and the target robotic arm is absent from the collection loop itself. 
Consequently, the resulting \(\mathcal{D}\) for robotic arm learning often exhibits a substantial sim-to-real gap.

\subsubsection{Automatic Data Generation}\label{sec:auto_data_gen}

Build on existing environments mentioned before. 
Automatic data generation synthesizes \(\mathcal{D}\) by synthesizing different parts of the structured record of embodied execution. 
Based on the content of this structured record,
we discuss automatic data generation in three forms: scene generation synthesizes sensory observations, 
demonstration synthesis generates sequences of motion together with contact events, and generative world models synthesize state transitions along with their future rollouts.
Scene generation synthesizes sensory observations by widening the scene and semantic conditions under which they are produced within the observation space.
ProcTHOR~\cite{deitke2022procthor} generated large-scale procedural indoor environments, 
Holodeck~\cite{yang2024holodeck} added language-conditioned semantic control, 
and RoboGen~\cite{wang2024robogen} connected asset retrieval, scene construction, and policy training within one automated pipeline.
Demonstration synthesis generates sequences of motion together with contact events by generating additional robotic arm trajectories from limited source demonstrations or task specifications.
MimicGen~\cite{mandlekar2023mimicgen} showed that one demonstration can be expanded into many valid trajectories through spatial transformation, 
and GenSim~\cite{wang2024gensim} moved this idea to task generation by using LLMs to produce simulation code and reward structure.
Generative world models synthesize state transitions along with their future rollouts by learning predictive models that can synthesize additional transitions from existing data.
Cosmos~\cite{nvidia2025cosmos} represents the internet-scale version of this idea, training video foundation models on web data to learn physical priors. 
IRASim~\cite{zhu2025irasim} grounds this in robot-specific interaction video, 
and DreamGen uses learned world models as both data generators and training environments~\cite{jang2025dreamge}. 
Automatic data generation, however, synthesizes \(\mathcal{D}\) by recombining or rolling forward an existing environment, the errors and limitations of that environment are also amplified.

\subsection{Data Refinement}\label{sec:data_refinement}

In real-world settings, available \(\mathcal{D}\) is often still limited in scale and uneven in quality., which motivates further data refinement and augmentation. 
We organize these methods around four mechanisms: visual and viewpoint augmentation (Section~\ref{sec:trajectory_redrawing}) broadens sensory observations, 
physically grounded augmentation (Section~\ref{sec:dynamics_constraints}) preserves the executability of sequences of motion together with contact events under kinematic and contact constraints, 
correction and recovery augmentation (Section~\ref{sec:correction_recovery}) supplements state transitions with the failure-recovery data absent from expert-only datasets, 
and data curation (Section~\ref{sec:data_curation}) determines whether existing \(\mathcal{D}\) still usable for learning and how low-quality data can be filtered, reweighted, or curated. 

\subsubsection{Visual and Viewpoint Augmentation}\label{sec:trajectory_redrawing}

Visual and viewpoint augmentation expands existing demonstrations along two main axes: semantic appearance variation and camera transformation, both without requiring new robot rollouts. 
Early work such as GenAug and ROSIE used generative image models to vary textures, objects, and scene composition in order to widen the visual distribution seen during imitation learning~\cite{chen2023genaug, yu2023scalingrobotlearningsemantically}. 
Later methods moved from appearance randomization to viewpoint-aware synthesis: RoVi-Aug augments both robot appearance and camera pose while preserving action labels and contact consistency under novel views~\cite{chen2025roviaug}. 
A complementary line extends supervision beyond robot demonstrations: VISTA enables zero-shot novel-view synthesis for view-invariant policy learning, H2R converts egocentric human video into robot-centric pretraining data by replacing hands with rendered end effectors, and LucidSim uses generative visuals coupled with physics simulation to synthesize first-person data for RGB sim-to-real transfer~\cite{wang2024vista, li2026h2r, yu2024lucidsim}.

\subsubsection{Physically Grounded Augmentation}\label{sec:dynamics_constraints}

Augmenting physical trajectories (e.g., shifting a grasp 5\,cm laterally) requires that the result still obey kinematic limits, collision constraints, and contact dynamics. 
CP-Gen~\cite{lin2025cpgen} integrates kinematic boundaries with contact dynamics for one-shot trajectory augmentation. 
yang et al.~\cite{yang2025physicsdriven} combines VR human demonstrations, retargeting, and trajectory optimization to maintain dynamic feasibility across embodiments. 
CyberDemo~\cite{wang2024cyberdemo} transfers simulated human demonstrations through large-scale in-simulator augmentation to real-world dexterous policies with minimal real data. 
Beyond geometric feasibility, RoCoDA~\cite{ameperosa2025rocoda} uses symmetry to distinguish perturbations that should leave the policy invariant from those that should induce equivariant action changes.

\subsubsection{Correction and Recovery Augmentation}\label{sec:correction_recovery}

Expert-only datasets lack failure-recovery examples, leaving policies brittle at out-of-distribution states. 
DMD~\cite{zhang2024dmd} uses diffusion models to synthesize corrective observations from perturbed states, 
enabling DAgger-style recovery without physical collection. Complementing these offline methods, 
IntervenGen~\cite{pinto2024intervengen} and CR-DAgger~\cite{xu2025compliant} systematize online human intervention during autonomous rollouts, converting sparse operator corrections into dense recovery-aware training signal.

\subsubsection{Data Curation and Verification}\label{sec:data_curation}

As datasets scale to millions of heterogeneous trajectories, the bottleneck shifts from quantity to quality. Existing methods operate at three main granularities: dataset-level filtering or reweighting to suppress noisy and suboptimal demonstrations, as in Re-Mix~\cite{hejna2025remix}; segment- or transition-level pruning to remove redundancy and preserve informative or long-tail samples, as in SCIZOR~\cite{zhang2025scizor}; and policy-aware valuation methods that score data by downstream utility, as in CUPID~\cite{agia2025cupidcuratingdatarobot}. 

\subsection{Data Pipeline}\label{sec:data_factories_robotic_arms}

In current practice, \(\mathcal{D}\) is increasingly managed over a unified lifecycle, where collection, generation, augmentation, and refinement are treated as parts of one integrated production lifecycle.
Existing systems reflect different ways of organizing this full lifecycle of \(\mathcal{D}\). 
RoboTwin~\cite{mu2025robotwin} keeps synthetic data tethered to measured physical setups by matching dual-arm kinematics and sensor noise, and its second version adds MLLM-driven task synthesis for automatic scenario generation. 
RoboVerse~\cite{geng2025roboverse} addresses fragmentation across simulators and datasets by providing a standardized MetaSim backbone spanning many tasks and assets. 
At larger scale, AgiBot World Colosseo~\cite{agibot2025} couples fleet-scale humanoid collection with simulator-based augmentation so that real and synthetic streams remain mutually useful, 
while NVIDIA Isaac GR00T~\cite{nvidia_gr00t} packages synthetic data generation, foundation-model training, and sim-to-real deployment into a humanoid-oriented stack built on Isaac infrastructure. 
Supporting these pipelines, AIRSPEED~\cite{xia2024airspeed} decouples collection and generation from specific hardware backends, 
LeRobot~\cite{lerobot2024} turns fragmented real-world trajectories into reusable repositories, 
and MimicLabs~\cite{saxena2025matters} studies how quantity, diversity, and quality trade off as pipelines scale.
Taken together, these infrastructures move \(\mathcal{D}\) production toward automated and reproducible workflows.

\section{Learning Paradigms of Robotic Arms}\label{sec:embodiment_method}

The data ecosystem discussed in Section~\ref{sec:embodiment_dataset} provides the knowledge support \(\mathcal{K}\) to the system. 
The present section examines how different learning paradigms organize \(\mathcal{K}\) into manipulation capabilities.
We divide the learning stack into two components. Cognitive planning, which we refer to as the embodied brain and discuss in Section~\ref{sec:embodied_brain}, converts semantic intent into structured planning artifacts. 
Policy learning, which we refer to as the embodied cerebellum and discuss in Section~\ref{sec:policy_learning_low_level}, maps observations to motor commands through different action representations.
Section~\ref{sec:integration_models} then examines how planning and policy learning are coupled in unified, hierarchical, and world-state-maintaining architectures, and how these design patterns relate to different data tiers.
Finally, Section~\ref{sec:deployment_pipeline} traces the deployment pipeline as an engineering concern that cuts across all three patterns. 
Figure~\ref{fig:learning_paradigms} illustrates the overall organization of learning paradigms.

\begin{figure}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,trim=2.7cm 4.3cm 2.2cm 1cm,clip]{assets/learningparadigms.pdf}
  \caption{Overview of learning paradigms for robotic arm manipulation. The figure organizes the pipeline into cognitive planning, policy learning, integration models, and deployment stages, linked by multimodal inputs and downstream control across robotic arm embodiments.}
  \Description{A diagram showing the organization of learning paradigms for robotic manipulation. The top level shows cognitive planning methods ranging from semantic logic to generative dynamics. The middle level shows policy learning approaches (P1 through P3) covering explicit continuous, discrete action, and iterative generative methods. The bottom level shows integration architectures (Type I, II, III) that couple planning and policy. Arrows indicate connections between data tiers, learning paradigms, and deployment stages.}\label{fig:learning_paradigms}
\end{figure}

\subsection{Cognitive Planning: The Embodied Brain}\label{sec:embodied_brain}

Cognitive planning bridges the gap between abstract human intent and the concrete specifications that downstream controllers require. 
As multimodal models become increasingly capable, the central question for embodied manipulation is not only semantic competence, but what execution-relevant form that competence should take.
We therefore classify planning methods into five paradigms (B1--B5) according to the primary artifact they produce for execution. 
B1 outputs executable semantic specifications.
B2 organizes intent into logical task structures.
B3 grounds intent in embodied context through intermediate task representations.
B4 outputs spatial representations for direct execution.
B5 evaluates actions through predictive rollouts of future dynamics.
Taken together, these paradigms reflect parallel routes by which high-level semantic competence is transformed into increasingly physically grounded representations for execution.
Table~\ref{tab:brain_paradigms} summarizes the paradigm-level properties and representative methods.

\subsubsection{Semantic Logic and Code Generation}\label{sec:code_gen}

B1 is closest to the semantic interface of MLLMs, outputting executable specifications such as code, reward functions, or skill rankings. 
Early work framed this as probabilistic alignment between language and affordance. 
SayCan~\cite{brohan2023can} grounds abstract plans by scoring primitive skills against both semantic relevance and execution feasibility, but remains limited to atomic actions without compositional logic. 
Program synthesis goes further. CaP~\cite{liang2022code} generates executable Python from natural language, and ProgPrompt~\cite{singh2022progprompt} embeds precondition assertions directly into the prompt structure to enforce logical constraints. 
A parallel line generates objective functions and leaves motor optimization to downstream solvers; Language to Reward~\cite{tan2023l2r} synthesizes programmatic reward functions that decouple semantic task specification from continuous trajectory optimization. 
Text2Touch~\cite{field2025text2touch} extends this reward-synthesis paradigm to tactile dexterous manipulation, using LLM-designed reward functions over tactile and proprioceptive signals for real-world in-hand control.
For structured control flow, LLM-BT~\cite{zhou2024llmbt} generates behavior trees that can be dynamically updated under environmental changes, enabling adaptive execution and recovery. 
LLM-as-BT~\cite{ao2025llmbt} generates modular behavior trees that support reactive switching and error recovery. 
Demo2Code~\cite{wang2023demo2code} extends the input modality by translating visual demonstrations into symbolic code, grounding program synthesis in physical observation alongside language. 
A shared limitation of B1 methods is that their outputs remain in the semantic layer and do not engage with the geometric or dynamic structure of the physical world; execution quality depends entirely on the downstream skill API or solver.

\subsubsection{Long-Horizon Task Decomposition}\label{sec:task_decomp}

B2 converts intent into logical task structures, decomposing long-horizon goals into ordered sub-task sequences or formal plans. 
LLM+P~\cite{liu2023llmp} addresses this by translating natural language into PDDL and delegating reasoning to classical solvers that guarantee feasibility. 
L3M+P~\cite{agarwal2025l3mp} further enables the solver to learn domain transition models from experience, reducing reliance on pre-defined rules.  
Classical solvers, however, face combinatorial explosions in large environments. SayPlan~\cite{rana2023sayplan} mitigates this by anchoring search in 3D scene graphs, hierarchically pruning irrelevant regions before planning.
Inner Monologue~\cite{huang2023inner} extends this paradigm to feedback-driven replanning, while Statler~\cite{yoneda2024statler} maintains a task-relevant world state throughout execution.

\subsubsection{Grounded Multimodal Representation}\label{sec:multimodal_reasoning}

B3 captures the regime in which multimodal models ground task intent in the current embodied scene, forming a scene-conditioned task representation that supports downstream policy execution.
PaLM-E~\cite{driess2023palme} injects visual observations and continuous robot representation directly into the LLM embedding space, so that semantic reasoning is conditioned on the current embodied scene.
EmbodiedGPT~\cite{mu2023embodiedgpt} extends this interface into the temporal domain by reasoning step by step with current observations in the loop, making task progression and intermediate sub-goals more explicit.
ECoT~\cite{zawalski2025ecot} externalizes richer intermediate traces, including task descriptions, object grounding, and step-level plans, before action prediction.
RoboBrain~\cite{ji2025robobrain} pushes this paradigm toward a more integrated manipulation-oriented interface by jointly modeling planning, affordance, trajectory, and longer-horizon visual context.
As the field broadens, B3 is increasingly evolving toward more integrated forms, with its implicit grounded representations being absorbed into larger models that jointly handle reasoning, grounding, memory, and action.

\begin{figure}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,trim=1cm 0cm 0cm 0.8cm,clip]{assets/brain.pdf}
  \caption{Cognitive planning paradigms for embodied manipulation. The figure summarizes five representative planning modes: semantic logic and code generation, long-horizon task decomposition, grounded multimodal reasoning, geometric grounding and spatial intelligence, and generative dynamics and physical imagination.}

A central brain icon is surrounded by five labeled panels: semantic logic and code generation, long-horizon task decomposition, grounded multimodal reasoning, geometric grounding and spatial intelligence, and generative dynamics and physical imagination. The panels use a broccoli-into-pot task to show how each paradigm represents planning differently, from symbolic code and step decomposition to affordance maps, point clouds, and imagined future states.}\label{fig:brain_paradigms}
\end{figure}

\subsubsection{Geometric Grounding and Spatial Intelligence}\label{sec:affordance_mapping}

B4 outputs spatial representations, including contact regions, SE(3) poses, affordance maps, and neural fields, that planners and controllers can consume directly. ManipLLM~\cite{li2024manipllm} predicts pixel-wise contact masks from language, and A3VLM~\cite{huang2024a3vlm} extends this grounding to articulated objects by inferring joint axes and motion types. In image space, CLIPort~\cite{shridhar2022cliport} establishes the two-stream formulation separating semantic understanding from spatial precision, while PASG~\cite{zhu2025pasg} strengthens the mapping with functional keypoints. In 3D, F3RM~\cite{shen2023distilled} injects open-vocabulary semantics into neural feature fields, while D3Fields~\cite{wang2025d3fields} extends this line with dynamic 3D descriptor fields that encode semantic features and instance masks and support goal specification from 2D images. VoxPoser~\cite{huang2023voxposer} and ReKep~\cite{huang2024rekep} push this line further by turning language or task descriptions into explicit value maps and time-varying keypoint constraints for downstream optimization. 
Across these methods, the common objective is to expose enough spatial structure for manipulation to query and optimize at execution time. 
A recurring limitation across many of these methods is that they emphasize the current geometric state more than post-contact evolution, leaving dynamic interaction and longer-horizon consequences to other modules.

\subsubsection{Generative Dynamics and Physical Imagination}\label{sec:generative_dynamics}

B5 comprises predictive planning artifacts, in which candidate actions are evaluated through explicit forecasts or imagined rollouts of future states, observations, or latent world dynamics. One route uses learned predictive models as the substrate for imagination. UniPi~\cite{du2023unipi} reformulates policy as future video generation, UWM~\cite{zhu2025uwm} unifies video and action diffusion within one transformer, and V-JEPA2~\cite{assran2025vjepa} shifts from pixel reconstruction to latent predictive reasoning. Purely learned prediction, however, drifts from physical consistency over long horizons. A second line constrains prediction with structural priors. Schulze et al.~\cite{schulze2025lagrangian} inject analytical mechanics into learned dynamics, Zhang et al.~\cite{zhang2025particlegrid} targets deformable interaction through particle-mesh structure, and LUMOS~\cite{nematollahi2025lumos} conditions predictive rollouts on language-level task compliance. Taken together, these methods define B5 by a shared commitment to evaluating action through predicted consequences, but they also expose its main difficulty: prediction becomes useful for planning only when it remains sufficiently stable under long-horizon physical interaction.

\subsection{Policy Learning: The Embodied Cerebellum}\label{sec:policy_learning_low_level}

Policy learning maps perceptual observations to motor commands. We organize methods into three families by how the action space is represented, not by whether sequence context or temporal structure is used internally. 
P1 (Explicit Continuous) policies output continuous action vectors via regression in a single forward pass; 
P2 (Discrete Action) policies quantize actions into tokens and generate them autoregressively via cross-entropy; 
P3 (Iterative Generative) policies model the full action distribution through multi-step denoising or flow matching. 
These three policy families differ mainly in how they balance action expressiveness against inference efficiency. 
Efficiency-oriented acceleration and compression are treated as a cross-cutting deployment concern in Section~\ref{sec:deployment_pipeline}. Table~\ref{tab:policy_paradigms} summarizes the paradigm-level properties.

\begin{figure}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,trim=3cm 0.5cm 3.5cm 0.3cm,clip]{assets/celeberrum.pdf}
  \caption{Comparison of policy learning paradigms for robotic manipulation. The figure contrasts explicit continuous policies, discrete-action policies, and iterative generative policies in terms of distributional expressivity and computational complexity.}\label{fig:policy_paradigms}
  \Description{A comparison diagram of three policy learning paradigms for robotic manipulation. The figure is arranged along two axes: computational complexity increases from left to right, and distribution expressivity increases from top to bottom. The top row compares explicit continuous policies and discrete-action policies, while the bottom row illustrates iterative generative policies based on diffusion and flow matching.}
\end{figure}

\subsubsection{Explicit Continuous Policies}\label{sec:explicit_continuous_policies}

P1 policies map observations to continuous action vectors in a single forward pass, trading distributional expressiveness for inference speed. 
The basic design separates a pre-trained visual encoder from a lightweight action head. 
BC-Z~\cite{jang2022bc} uses this pattern for multi-task zero-shot generalization, MVP~\cite{xiao2022mvp} shows that masked visual pre-training yields representations robust enough to reduce the policy to a simple MLP, and PointPolicy~\cite{haldar2025pointpolicy} extends explicit regression to 3D point clouds. 
Single-step prediction, however, ignores trajectory-level context. Decision Transformer~\cite{chen2021decisiontransformer} instead reframes control as autoregressive sequence modeling with a regression objective, while ACT-style methods~\cite{zhao2023learning, fu2024mobile} combine action chunking with CVAE-based modeling to predict short-horizon action sequences and reduce jitter in bimanual and mobile manipulation.
BAKU~\cite{haldar2024baku} combines CNN-based feature extraction with a transformer policy for multi-task settings. 
To handle distribution shift, MimicPlay~\cite{wang2023mimicplay} learns goal-conditioned behaviors from play data, Iterative Residual Policy~\cite{chi2022iterativeresidualpolicy} predicts residual corrections to recover from execution drift, and Motion Tracks~\cite{ren2025motion_tracks} learns a unified motion representation for cross-embodiment adaptation. 
Despite their simplicity and efficiency, explicit continuous policies are poorly matched to multimodal action distributions, as a single deterministic prediction cannot capture multiple valid behaviors.

\subsubsection{Discrete Action Policies}\label{sec:discrete_action_policies}

P2 policies discretize continuous actions into tokens and model them autoregressively with cross-entropy objectives, enabling multimodal action generation beyond unimodal regression.
Early approaches employ uniform discretization. 
RT-1~\cite{brohan2023rt1} maps continuous dimensions to 256 bins, Gato~\cite{reed2022generalistagent} uses $\mu$-law encoding to tokenize diverse modalities into a unified sequence, and PerAct~\cite{shridhar2022peract} extends this to 3D by classifying actions over voxelized grids.
However, fixed discretization imposes a rigid token space that is often poorly matched to continuous action distributions, motivating learned discretization methods that adapt the token space to the data.
Learned discretization addresses this limitation. BeT~\cite{shafiullah2022bet} derives action modes through k-means clustering, VQ-BeT~\cite{lee2024vqbet} learns a compact discrete action vocabulary with VQ-VAE, and CARP~\cite{gong2025carp} further improves precision through coarse-to-fine hierarchical prediction.
Once actions are reliably tokenized, they can be generated within the same autoregressive framework used for text. VIMA~\cite{jiang2023vima} interleaves visual objects with text instructions for multimodal prompt conditioning, GR-1~\cite{wu2023gr1} unifies vision, language, and action in a single stream, Seer~\cite{tian2024seer} attributes much of the gain to the discrete formulation itself, and ARP~\cite{zhang2025arp} shows that chunk-level autoregressive generation generalizes across diverse robots. ICIL~\cite{fu2025icil} further treats imitation as sequence completion, adapting to unseen tasks by conditioning on demonstration prompts without gradient updates.
The central challenge in these methods lies in aligning discrete action tokens with the structured, continuous nature of robot actions, while preserving action fidelity and efficient autoregressive generation.

\subsubsection{Iterative Generative Policies}\label{sec:iterative_generative_policies}

P3 policies treat action prediction as conditional generative modeling over continuous action trajectories, using iterative refinement to approximate the full action distribution.
Early diffusion-based formulations first appeared in planning and structured generation: Diffuser~\cite{janner2022diffuser} casts long-horizon trajectory generation as probabilistic inpainting, and StructDiffusion~\cite{liu2022structdiffusion} constructs physically valid multi-object configurations from language prompts. 
Diffusion Policy~\cite{chi2023diffusion} subsequently brings this paradigm into mainstream visuomotor control by learning the score function of the action distribution, thereby avoiding the mode-averaging limitations of direct regression. 
In 3D embodied manipulation, DP3~\cite{ze2024diffusion} further conditions diffusion on point-cloud observations for spatial grounding, while iDP3~\cite{liang2024idp3} extends this formulation with improved 3D representations for more robust deployment.
Despite these advances, iterative denoising still imposes substantial inference latency.
Flow matching addresses this by learning velocity fields that transport noise to data along straighter trajectories with fewer integration steps. 
Consistency Policy~\cite{prasad2024consistency} distills a teacher diffusion model into few-step generation, Streaming Flow Policy~\cite{jiang2025streaming} streams action trajectories to the robot during generation, and RecFlow~\cite{xue2025recflow} accelerates inference by rectifying the learned flow itself.
At foundation-model scale, Octo~\cite{octomodelteam2024} trains a transformer-based diffusion policy on the Open X-Embodiment dataset for cross-embodiment manipulation, RDT-1B~\cite{liu2025rdtb} scales to the 1B-parameter regime on larger multi-robot data, and FLOWER~\cite{reuss2025flower} improves sample efficiency through flow-based acceleration.
Overall, P3 policies offer the strongest distributional modeling capacity, but their practicality depends on making iterative generative control efficient enough for deployment while preserving action quality, control stability, and manageable compute cost.

\subsection{Integration Models}\label{sec:integration_models}

After discussing cognitive planning and policy learning separately, we now consider how cognitive planning and policy learning are integrated into complete manipulation systems. RT-2~\cite{brohan2023rt2} showed that semantic understanding, reasoning, and action generation can be combined within a single model, opening a design space that has since developed along three architectural directions.

We classify integration architectures by their primary structural commitment, systems with mixed characteristics are assigned to the type that best reflects their dominant design choice. Type~I emphasizes end-to-end unification within a shared backbone. Type~II covers hierarchical designs that separate slower reasoning from faster execution. Type III maintains a shared world representation—spatial, predictive, or both—that mediates between planning and policy.

Within this three-type taxonomy, several recurrent patterns appear repeatedly in practice. Type~II includes both explicit hierarchy, where reasoning and control are linked through language, symbolic structure, or task traces, and implicit hierarchy, where the practical split is clear but the interface remains latent or only lightly structured. Type III likewise includes both spatial grounding and predictive world models, depending on whether the maintained representation is primarily geometric, predictive, or both. Available data regimes do not determine these patterns uniquely, but they do make some of them easier to support than others. Table~\ref{tab:data_learning_map} summarizes these recurrent patterns, and Table~\ref{tab:embodied_models_2026_v4} surveys representative systems across the three main types.

\subsubsection{Type I\@: Unified End-to-End Foundation Models}

Type I models treat actions as another modality tokenized alongside text and image patches, absorbing semantic understanding, reasoning, and action generation into a single backbone. 
Early systems discretized actions into language-like tokens. RT-2~\cite{brohan2023rt2} co-fine-tuned a vision-language model to output discrete action tokens, and OpenVLA~\cite{kim2024openvla} released a 7B model trained on 970k Open X-Embodiment trajectories. X-VLA~\cite{zheng2026xvla} continues this line with embodiment-specific soft prompts inside a shared transformer, showing how a unified backbone can extend across multiple robot platforms without introducing an explicit hierarchical split. Continuous representations followed. $\pi_0$~\cite{black2024pi0} introduced flow matching for high-frequency dexterous control, and RDT-2~\cite{liu2026rdt2} extends this to bimanual manipulation with zero-shot cross-embodiment deployment. RoboMamba~\cite{liu2024robomamba} replaces self-attention with State Space Models for linear-time processing, and auxiliary reconstructive objectives encode 3D spatial structure to compensate for weak explicit geometry~\cite{song2025reconvla}.

Although Type I systems demonstrate strong benefits from unifying perception, semantic reasoning, and action generation within a single architecture, this unification also comes at a cost.
By forcing cognitive planning and motor control into the same representational and computational loop, Type I couples processes that operate at very different temporal and physical scales. 
As a result, its central challenge is whether a single unified architecture can effectively support both high-level reasoning and real-time control.

\subsubsection{Type II\@: Hierarchical \& Modular Reasoning Models}

Type II models decouple slow cognitive planning from fast policy execution. One branch uses explicit hierarchy. 
RT-H~\cite{belkhale2024rth} introduced language motions as an intermediate layer between high-level instructions and low-level actions, CoT-VLA~\cite{zhao2025cotvla} and ThinkAct~\cite{huang2025thinkact} expose intermediate reasoning steps, RoBridge~\cite{zhang2025robridge} places a symbolic bridge between a high-level planner and an embodied agent, and HAMSTER~\cite{li2025hamster} predicts a coarse 2D path that explicitly guides a low-level 3D-aware control policy.
Another branch uses implicit hierarchy, where the practical split is clear but the interface remains latent or only lightly structured. 
FiS-VLA~\cite{chen2025fis} couples a slower semantic branch with a real-time execution branch, Hi Robot~\cite{shi2025hirobot} employs a System 1/System 2 hierarchy for open-ended instructions and real-time human feedback, and GO-1~\cite{agibot2025} scales the same design to industrial bimanual control.

Although explicit and implicit Type II models offer advantages, their drawbacks are also evident.
Explicit hierarchies risk overly rigid and lossy intermediate representations, while implicit hierarchies risk opacity, weak supervision, and cross-level misalignment. 
What they share is the bottleneck introduced by decomposition: once slow planning is separated from fast control, the planner-controller interface becomes the critical constraint on information flow, error correction, and coordination across levels.

\subsubsection{Type III\@: Spatial Grounding \& Predictive World Models}

Type III maintains a shared world representation that planning and policy jointly consume. 
This world state takes two forms. 
Spatial grounding systems build a persistent geometric representation: 3D-VLA~\cite{zhen20243dvla} embeds 3D perception into the LLM for spatial reasoning, SpatialVLA~\cite{qu2025spatialvla} grounds actions in metric 3D structure via Ego3D Position Encoding, TraceVLA~\cite{zheng2025tracevla} conditions generation on visual trajectory traces, and BridgeVLA~\cite{li2025bridgevla} aligns spatial and predictive representations to ensure kinematic feasibility. 
Predictive world models instead learn forward dynamics from data: VideoVLA~\cite{shen2025videovla} jointly predicts action sequences and future visual outcomes using a pre-trained video generator, DreamVLA~\cite{zhang2025dreamvla} integrates a diffusion-based world model into the manipulation pipeline for planning via imagined trajectories, and DreamZero~\cite{ye2026dreamzero} jointly models video and action as a World Action Model.

Although Type III models exhibit strong potential, they rely heavily on a reliable shared world representation. 
If this representation is inaccurate or stale, the entire system can be misled. 
While correction is possible, it often incurs substantial computational and engineering cost. 
In addition, Type III models typically demand large-scale data and resources to train, and still face significant bottlenecks in real-world deployment.

\subsection{Deployment Pipeline}\label{sec:deployment_pipeline}

Following these advances in embodied system design, the field has encountered a new set of challenges in deploying such systems on physical hardware. 
We discuss deployment in three stages: offline pre-deployment (Section~\ref{sec:pre_deployment}), online physical alignment (Section~\ref{sec:online_alignment}), and inference-time execution (Section~\ref{sec:inference_execution}). 
These stages are analytically distinct but tightly coupled in practice: pre-deployment choices constrain later adaptation, and online alignment reshapes the execution properties that inference-time control must sustain. 
Table~\ref{tab:deployment_lifecycle} summarizes the main bottlenecks and representative techniques at each stage.

\subsubsection{Stage 1: Offline Pre-deployment}\label{sec:pre_deployment}

Before physical deployment, the main challenges are transferring policies from simulation to reality, generalizing across embodiments, and making large models practical for real-world execution.
Q-Transformer~\cite{chebotar2023qtransformer} shows that autoregressive Q-functions can scale offline actor-critic learning to transformer policies through temporal difference backups on static datasets.
X-Sim~\cite{dan2025xsim} constructs embodiment-agnostic representations through a real-to-sim-to-real pipeline that establishes shared latent action spaces across platforms. 
ASAP~\cite{he2025asap} addresses automated physics alignment by learning a delta-action compensation model that corrects simulation-to-reality dynamics mismatch. 
TRANSIC~\cite{jiang2025transic} takes a complementary human-in-the-loop approach, allowing operators to intervene during real-world execution and correct failures online. 
Residual policies learned from these corrections are then combined with the simulation-trained policy for autonomous deployment.
As model size grows, pre-deployment compression also becomes necessary: FAST~\cite{pertsch2025fast} introduces DCT-based action tokenization, replacing per-dimension discretization with a frequency-space representation for more efficient deployment.

\subsubsection{Stage 2: Online Physical Alignment}\label{sec:online_alignment}

Once deployed on physical hardware, the primary bottleneck shifts from transfer preparation to adaptation under real contact, friction, sensing noise, and out-of-distribution objects. 
SimpleVLA-RL~\cite{li2026simplevlarl} shows that large VLA models can be fine-tuned directly through large-scale reinforcement learning, FLaRe~\cite{hu2024flare} addresses the tension between generalist priors and specialist performance through adaptive large-scale RL fine-tuning, while DiWA~\cite{chandra2025diwa} adapts diffusion policies through an offline world model, reducing the amount of physical interaction needed for post-training improvement. 
ConRFT~\cite{chen2025conrft} combines offline BC/Q-learning with online consistency policies and human intervention checkpoints to support safe adaptation in contact-rich settings. Human-in-the-loop real-world reinforcement learning provides another effective route for this stage: HIL-SERL~\cite{luo2025humaninloop} combines demonstrations, learned reward modeling, and online human corrections to learn precise and dexterous manipulation skills directly on physical robots.
VT-Refine~\cite{huang2025vtrefine} further demonstrates that visuo-tactile policies, when paired with tactile digital twins and simulation-guided refinement, can improve high-precision dual-arm coordination. Other alignment strategies include residual adaptation via off-policy RL~\cite{xiao2025pld}. The adaptation choices made here directly affect Stage~3: aggressive full-parameter fine-tuning can improve task performance but complicate compression and serving, whereas residual or modular updates preserve more of the original policy structure at the cost of additional execution-time overhead.

\subsubsection{Stage 3: Inference-Time Execution}\label{sec:inference_execution}

During real-world deployment, the key challenge is to sustain reliable closed-loop execution on resource-constrained edge hardware.
QVLA~\cite{xu2026qvla} addresses action-sensitive quantization by allocating precision unevenly across channels, recognizing that action-critical dimensions tolerate less compression than language-oriented ones. 
TinyVLA~\cite{wen2025tinyvla} targets fast and data-efficient vision-language-action modeling for robotic manipulation, while PD-VLA~\cite{pdvla2025} accelerates inference by replacing autoregressive decoding with parallel fixed-point iterations over action chunks.
Runtime robustness is equally critical. Policy Contrastive Decoding~\cite{wu2026policycontrastive} reduces spurious visual dependence during action generation, FAIL-Detect~\cite{xu2025detectfailures} detects potential failures under out-of-distribution conditions, and Neural MP~\cite{dalal2024neuralmp} augments learned policies with motion-planning-based safety constraints.
Reflective TTP~\cite{hong2026rttp} supports online recovery through reflection and test-time updates, re-evaluating actions with hindsight to adapt the policy during deployment.

Despite progress across all these three stages, current methods still face several limitations. 
Pre-deployment approaches often remain sensitive to simulator fidelity, offline data coverage, and the scope of embodiment variation, which limits the robustness of transfer to open-ended real-world settings. 
Online physical alignment methods improve adaptation under real dynamics, but they often require expensive robot interaction, careful reward or intervention design, and, in many cases, substantial human involvement that may hinder scalability. 
At inference time, compression, quantization, and decoding acceleration improve deployability, yet their impact on fine-grained action fidelity and contact-rich control remains insufficiently understood.

\section{System Evaluation of Robotic Arms}\label{sec:evaluation}

Sections~\ref{sec:embodiment_hardware}--\ref{sec:embodiment_method} examined how robotic manipulation systems are shaped by embodiment, data, learning, and deployment. 
This chapter closes that loop by evaluating the resulting systems from four complementary perspectives.
Section~\ref{sec:eval_task_execution} focuses on task execution quality, Section~\ref{sec:eval_data_sample} on data efficiency and quality, Section~\ref{sec:eval_benchmarking} on generalization and transfer, and Section~\ref{sec:eval_energy} on deployment efficiency and cost. 
Table~\ref{tab:eval_metrics} summarizes representative metrics and protocols.
